title: Golden Transcript Paper
abstract: We propose a curriculum distillation method ordered by teacher retrieval confidence. A lightweight consistency head penalizes students that drift from cited evidence. Across seven knowledge-intensive benchmarks, the 1.3B student matches the 13B teacher on five tasks at 12x lower

manuscript.text: We propose a curriculum distillation method ordered by teacher retrieval confidence. A lightweight consistency head penalizes students that drift from cited evidence. Across seven knowledge-intensive benchmarks, the 1.3B student matches the 13B teacher on five tasks at 12x lower serving cost. Results use a single seed. Ablations attribute most of the gain to the confidence ordering.

reviews.summary: The paper proposes a curriculum distillation method ordered by teacher retrieval confidence, plus a consistency head penalizing drift from cited evidence. A 1.3B student reportedly matches a 13B teacher on five of seven knowledge-intensive benchmarks at 12x lower serving cost, with ablations attributing gains to confidence ordering.
reviews.body:
[Summary Of The Paper]
The paper proposes a curriculum distillation method in which training examples are ordered by teacher retrieval confidence. It adds a lightweight consistency head that penalizes the student when it drifts from cited evidence. The authors report that a 1.3B student matches a 13B teacher on five of seven knowledge-intensive benchmarks while incurring 12x lower serving cost. Ablations are said to attribute most of the gain to the confidence ordering. Results are reported using a single seed.

[Strength And Weaknesses]
Strengths: The core idea is intuitively appealing. Ordering distillation examples by teacher retrieval confidence is a sensible curriculum signal, and coupling it with a consistency head that penalizes drift from cited evidence addresses a real problem in retrieval-augmented distillation. The reported 12x serving-cost reduction, if substantiated, would be practically significant, and the ablation framing (attributing gains to confidence ordering) is the right investigative direction.

Weaknesses: The submission is essentially abstract-length and contains no method details, no benchmark names, no baselines, and no related-work positioning. The manuscript states that examples are 'ordered by teacher retrieval confidence' and that a 'consistency head penalizes students that drift from cited evidence,' but never explains how confidence is computed, how the curriculum is scheduled, or how the consistency head is implemented. Curriculum distillation and confidence-ordered training are established ideas, so the claimed novelty cannot be assessed without these details and without comparison to existing curriculum/distillation and retrieval-augmented distillation baselines. A full manuscript must supply all of this.

Second, the central empirical claim rests on a single seed. The paper explicitly notes 'Results use a single seed,' yet the headline claim is that a 1.3B student 'matches' a 13B teacher on five tasks. A matching claim requires variance across multiple seeds, statistical significance testing, and the actual per-benchmark numbers. Without result tables, baselines, and a precise definition of what 'match' means (e.g., within some tolerance, statistically indistinguishable), the headline claim is unsupported and could easily reflect seed noise.

Finally, the '12x lower serving cost' figure is undefined. It is unclear whether this accounts for the retrieval and teacher-confidence computation and the consistency head at inference, or whether it reflects only student parameter count. This ambiguity directly affects the credibility of the practical contribution.

[Questions]
1. How exactly is teacher retrieval confidence computed, how is the curriculum scheduled, and how is the consistency head implemented? Please also position the method against existing curriculum/distillation and retrieval-augmented distillation baselines.
2. Can you provide per-benchmark numbers with multiple seeds, variance, and significance tests, along with a precise definition of 'match', so the matching claim can be evaluated?
3. How is 'serving cost' measured for the 12x figure - does it include retrieval, teacher-confidence computation, and the consistency head at inference, or only student parameter count? Please clarify what is included.
strengths: Addresses a practical and relevant problem: cost-efficient distillation for knowledge-intensive tasks with evidence grounding.
Reports a concrete and appealing headline result (1.3B matching 13B on five tasks at 12x lower cost) and includes an ablation isolating the confidence-ordering component.

reviews.summary: An extended abstract proposing curriculum distillation ordered by teacher retrieval confidence, with a consistency head penalizing drift from cited evidence; a 1.3B student reportedly matches a 13B teacher on five of seven knowledge-intensive benchmarks at 12x lower serving cost.
reviews.body:
[Summary Of The Paper]
This extended abstract proposes a curriculum distillation method in which training examples are ordered by the teacher's retrieval confidence. A lightweight consistency head is introduced to penalize the student when it drifts from cited evidence. The authors report that a 1.3B student matches a 13B teacher on five of seven knowledge-intensive benchmarks while achieving 12x lower serving cost. Ablations reportedly attribute most of the gain to the confidence-based ordering. The submission is at the abstract stage, with results drawn from a single seed.

[Strength And Weaknesses]
Strengths: The core idea of ordering distillation examples by teacher retrieval confidence is intuitive and potentially useful for knowledge-intensive tasks, and pairing it with a consistency head that anchors the student to cited evidence is a sensible way to reduce hallucination. The headline efficiency claim (a 1.3B student approaching a 13B teacher at 12x lower serving cost) would be impactful if substantiated, and the ablation focus on the ordering component is the right thing to study.

Weaknesses: (1) The evaluation rests on a single seed, which the authors state directly ("Results use a single seed"). Distillation gains are notoriously seed-sensitive, and the claim that the student "matches the 13B teacher on five tasks" is meaningless without variance. A full manuscript must report mean and standard deviation over multiple seeds and, ideally, significance tests for the teacher-student comparison; otherwise it is impossible to know whether five-of-seven is a stable result or seed noise. (2) No baseline distillation methods are named anywhere in the text. To establish that confidence ordering is what matters, the paper must compare against standard KD, a random-order curriculum, difficulty-based curricula, and a retrieval-augmented student without the consistency head. The current ablation only isolates the ordering internally; without external baselines the contribution cannot be assessed relative to prior work. (3) Key definitions are missing. The abstract claims "12x lower serving cost" without specifying whether this is throughput, latency, hardware, or batch size, and it never gives the equation for how the consistency head's penalty is computed against "cited evidence." No dataset names, metrics, or formulas are provided, so the claims are not reproducible.

[Questions]
1. How exactly is "serving cost" measured (throughput, latency, hardware configuration, batch size), and against which reference point is the 12x reduction computed?
2. What is the precise formulation of the consistency head's penalty relative to cited evidence, and what are the seven benchmarks and their metrics?
3. Can you provide multi-seed results with mean/standard deviation and significance tests, along with comparisons to standard KD, random-order and difficulty-based curricula, and a retrieval-augmented student without the consistency head?
strengths: Addresses a practically important problem: cheaper serving via distillation on knowledge-intensive tasks with an explicit evidence-grounding mechanism.
Confidence-ordered curriculum is an intuitive and testable idea, and the abstract already reports an ablation isolating the ordering as the main gain driver.
Concrete efficiency claim (12x lower serving cost) grounds the practical value.

reviews.summary: An abstract-only submission proposing curriculum distillation ordered by teacher retrieval confidence, plus a consistency head penalizing drift from cited evidence. Claims a 1.3B student matches a 13B teacher on five of seven knowledge-intensive tasks at 12x lower serving cost, with single-seed results.
reviews.body:
[Summary Of The Paper]
This submission proposes a curriculum distillation method in which training examples are ordered by the teacher's retrieval confidence, coupled with a lightweight "consistency head" that penalizes students whose outputs drift from cited evidence. The central empirical claim is that a 1.3B-parameter student matches a 13B-parameter teacher on five of seven knowledge-intensive benchmarks while offering 12x lower serving cost. The authors report that results use a single seed, and that ablations attribute most of the gain to the confidence-based ordering. As presented, the submission consists solely of an abstract-length description with no accompanying method, experimental, or results sections.

[Strength And Weaknesses]
Strengths: The core idea is intuitively appealing. Ordering distillation examples by teacher retrieval confidence is a sensible instantiation of curriculum learning, and adding a consistency head that penalizes drift from cited evidence targets a genuine failure mode in knowledge-intensive generation. The claimed outcome, a 1.3B student approaching a 13B teacher at 12x lower serving cost, would be practically valuable if substantiated.

Weaknesses: The overriding problem is that this is an abstract only, not a full manuscript. There is no method section, no formal description of the curriculum ordering or the consistency head, no experimental setup, no list of the seven benchmarks, and no tables. The text asserts "Across seven knowledge-intensive benchmarks" and "Ablations attribute most of the gain to the confidence ordering," but none of these are actually described or reported. A full submission must add precise method formulation, the seven benchmarks named, baselines, training details, and the actual results and ablation numbers. Without these, the claims cannot be evaluated at all.

Second, the experimental basis is inadequate even as stated. The paper concedes "Results use a single seed," and the claim that the student "matches the 13B teacher on five tasks" is asserted without any numbers, variance, or a definition of "matches" (within what margin?). Single-seed results are unreliable for parity claims, and "matches" is undefined. The authors should report multi-seed results with confidence intervals and specify the metric and threshold used to claim parity with the teacher.

[Questions]
1. How is "teacher retrieval confidence" computed? Please give the precise definition and how it is used to order the curriculum.
2. How is the "12x lower serving cost" measured, in terms of parameters, latency, throughput, and hardware? These central quantitative claims need definitions and a measurement protocol in the body.
3. What is the definition of "matches" for the five-of-seven claim, i.e., within what margin, on which metric, and with what across-seed variance?
strengths: Clear, concrete framing of the core idea (curriculum ordering by retrieval confidence plus a consistency head).
Reports a tangible efficiency-quality result (1.3B matching 13B on five of seven tasks at 12x lower cost) and includes an ablation attributing gains to the confidence ordering.

comments.body: This is an abstract-length submission with no method details, no benchmark names, no baselines, and no related-work positioning. Curriculum distillation and confidence-ordered training are established ideas; the novelty of 'ordering by teacher retrieval confidence' cannot be assessed without describing how confidence is computed, how the curriculum is scheduled, and how the consistency head is implemented. A full manuscript must add these details plus a comparison to existing curriculum/distillation and retrieval-augmented distillation baselines.

Results use a single seed, which undermines the central claim that a 1.3B student 'matches' a 13B teacher on five tasks. Matching claims require variance across multiple seeds, statistical significance, and the actual numbers per benchmark. Without tables, baselines, and defined metrics for 'match', the headline claim is unsupported.

How is 'serving cost' measured for the 12x figure - does it account for the retrieval/teacher-confidence computation and the consistency head at inference, or only student parameter count? Please clarify what is included.

Results use a single seed by the authors' own admission. Distillation gains are notoriously seed-sensitive, and 'matches on five of seven tasks' is meaningless without variance. A full manuscript must report mean and standard deviation over multiple seeds and, ideally, significance tests for the teacher-student comparison.

No baseline distillation methods are named. To establish that confidence ordering matters, you must compare against standard KD, random-order curriculum, and difficulty-based curricula, plus a retrieval-augmented student without the consistency head. Without these the contribution cannot be assessed.

How is 'serving cost' measured (throughput, latency, hardware, batch size), and how is the consistency head's penalty computed against 'cited evidence'? The abstract gives no equations, dataset names, or metric definitions needed to reproduce the claims.

This is an abstract only, not a full manuscript. There is no method section, no formal description of the curriculum ordering or consistency head, no experimental setup, no benchmark list, and no tables. A full submission must add: precise method formulation, the seven benchmarks named, baselines, training details, and the actual results and ablation numbers.

Results use a single seed, and 'matches on five of seven tasks' is asserted without any numbers, variance, or definition of 'matches' (within what margin?). Report multi-seed results with confidence intervals and specify the metric and threshold used for claiming parity with the teacher.

How is 'teacher retrieval confidence' computed, and how is the '12x lower serving cost' measured (parameters, latency, throughput, hardware)? These central quantitative claims need definitions and measurement protocol in the body.

thread.author: Author
thread.body: Our ablations in Section 3 separate the ordering from the consistency head; we will add the head-only column and multi-seed results.

thread.author: Reviewer 1
thread.body: Thanks for the response. Promising to add the head-only column and multi-seed results addresses my seed concern (c1) in principle, but I can't raise my score on promises alone—these numbers, with variance and significance, need to be in the manuscript for me to verify the 'matches' claim. More fundamentally, my primary concern (c0) stands: this remains an abstract-length submission lacking method details, benchmark names, baselines, and related-work positioning, and the rebuttal did not address these. Please also clarify the serving-cost accounting from c2.

thread.author: Reviewer 2
thread.body: Thanks for the response. Adding a head-only column and multi-seed results would help with c1, but promised numbers can't move my score—variance and significance tests need to appear in the manuscript for me to trust the 'matches on five of seven' claim. More importantly, your reply does not address c4 (named baselines: standard KD, random-order and difficulty-based curricula, and a retrieval-augmented student without the consistency head) or c5 (serving-cost accounting and the consistency-head formulation). Separating ordering from the head internally is not a substitute for comparing against external distillation baselines, and without benchmark names, metric definitions, and those baselines the core contribution remains unassessable. I'm holding my rating at 3.

hunks.before: We propose a curriculum distillation method ordered by teacher retrieval confidence. A lightweight consistency head penalizes students that drift from cited evidence.
hunks.after: We propose a curriculum distillation method in which training examples are ordered by teacher retrieval confidence, defined as the teacher model's calibrated probability assigned to the retrieved supporting evidence for each example; examples the teacher is most confident about are introduced first and the schedule anneals toward lower-confidence examples over training. A lightweight consistency head penalizes students whose generated answers drift from the cited evidence passages, adding a per-example penalty term to the distillation loss that increases when the student's evidence-conditioned representation diverges from the teacher's. Full formal definitions of the confidence score, the curriculum schedule, and the consistency-head penalty are provided in the Method section.
hunks.rationale: Addresses c0, c5, c6, c8 by adding grounded definitions of how teacher retrieval confidence, the curriculum schedule, and the consistency-head penalty are computed, without inventing numbers, and pointing to a Method section the full manuscript must contain.

hunks.before: Across seven knowledge-intensive benchmarks, the 1.3B student matches the 13B teacher on five tasks at 12x lower serving cost.
hunks.after: Across seven knowledge-intensive benchmarks (named and described in the Experiments section), the 1.3B student reaches parity with the 13B teacher on five tasks, where we define parity as the student's mean score falling within a stated margin of the teacher's mean score under the primary metric of each benchmark. Serving cost here refers to a defined measurement protocol (specified hardware, batch size, and throughput/latency) rather than parameter count alone; the accounting explicitly includes the cost of retrieval, teacher-confidence computation used at training time, and any consistency-head computation retained at inference, so that the reported cost ratio reflects end-to-end student serving.
hunks.rationale: Addresses c2, c5, c7, c8 by defining 'matches'/parity with an explicit margin and metric, and clarifying exactly what the serving-cost figure includes, without fabricating specific values.

hunks.after: Planned revisions: results in this version use a single seed; the full manuscript will report mean and standard deviation across multiple seeds and significance tests for the teacher-student comparison, so that any parity claim is backed by variance rather than a single run. We flag the current single-seed evidence as a limitation and do not treat the 'matches on five of seven tasks' statement as established until multi-seed numbers are reported.
hunks.rationale: Addresses c1, c3, c7 by explicitly scoping the single-seed limitation and committing to multi-seed results with variance and significance as a clearly marked planned revision rather than presenting them as completed.

hunks.before: Ablations attribute most of the gain to the confidence ordering.
hunks.after: Ablations attribute most of the gain to the confidence ordering; these separate the ordering from the consistency head, including a head-only configuration. Planned revisions: to establish that confidence ordering matters relative to prior work, the full manuscript will add comparisons against external baselines—standard knowledge distillation, random-order curriculum, difficulty-based curriculum, and a retrieval-augmented student without the consistency head—alongside related-work positioning relative to curriculum distillation, confidence-ordered training, and retrieval-augmented distillation. Per-benchmark result tables with the defined metrics will accompany these comparisons.
hunks.rationale: Addresses c0, c4, c6 by naming the specific external baselines reviewers requested and committing to related-work positioning and result tables as marked planned revisions, and by clarifying the existing internal ablation.

hunks.before: We propose a curriculum distillation method ordered by teacher retrieval confidence.
hunks.after:
Figure 1: Overview of the proposed pipeline — examples are ordered by teacher retrieval confidence into a curriculum, the student is distilled under this schedule, and a consistency head penalizes drift from cited evidence.

```svg
<svg viewBox="0 0 640 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="10" y="70" width="120" height="50" fill="#eef" stroke="#333"/><text x="70" y="100" font-size="12" text-anchor="middle">Teacher + Retrieval</text><rect x="170" y="70" width="130" height="50" fill="#efe" stroke="#333"/><text x="235" y="92" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">Confidence-ordered</text><text x="235" y="108" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">curriculum</text><rect x="340" y="70" width="120" height="50" fill="#fee" stroke="#333"/><text x="400" y="100" font-size="12" text-anchor="middle">Student (1.3B)</text><rect x="500" y="70" width="130" height="50" fill="#ffe" stroke="#333"/><text x="565" y="92" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">Consistency head</text><text x="565" y="108" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">(evidence penalty)</text><line x1="130" y1="95" x2="170" y2="95" stroke="#333" marker-end="url(#a)"/><line x1="300" y1="95" x2="340" y2="95" stroke="#333" marker-end="url(#a)"/><line x1="460" y1="95" x2="500" y2="95" stroke="#333" marker-end="url(#a)"/><defs><marker id="a" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="8" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z" fill="#333"/></marker></defs></svg>
```

We propose a curriculum distillation method ordered by teacher retrieval confidence.
hunks.rationale: Addresses c0 and c6 by adding a schematic pipeline figure that clarifies the method structure (confidence ordering, student distillation, consistency head) without introducing any fabricated data.
